title: The Antidote to Van Maren's Poison
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman's lengthy rejoinder to Jonathan Van Maren's partial response (and a follow-up Josh Brahm podcast) defending the Abolitionist Movement against accusations of historical illiteracy, theological error, and political naivete. Silberman walks through 23 numbered points, contrasting immediatism with pro-life incrementalism by re-litigating Wilberforce, Garrison, Clarkson, Dundas, the Dolben Act, criminalization of abortion, equal protection bills, and bloodguilt theology.
audiences: State legislator weighing an abolition bill

questions.question: What does Silberman say is the most reliable indicator that someone knows they are losing an argument?
questions.answer: Silberman flips Van Maren's own framing back on him: while avoiding key points is one tell, an even stronger tell is a pattern of dishonesty in debate. He claims Van Maren is establishing exactly that pattern.
questions.quote: Avoidance of important points is always a good indicator that someone knows they're wrong, as Van Maren astutely, yet obliviously pointed out. But an even greater indicator of someone who knows they're wrong is when they are consistently unable to engage in a debate without dishonesty. Van Maren is beginning to demonstrate an undeniable pattern of deception.

questions.question: How does Silberman defend Wilberforce's prioritizing the slave trade before emancipation against the charge that this made him an incrementalist?
questions.answer: He argues Wilberforce and Clarkson believed that criminalizing the trade would functionally kill the institution of slavery itself, citing Clarkson's 'axe at the very root' language and Wilberforce's 1807 'what shall we abolish next?' to show they treated the trade ban as the decisive blow, not as a regulatory compromise.
questions.quote: Manstealing was the foundation of slavery, and by criminalizing that act, the abolitionists believed they were delivering the death blow to slavery. In his article on the subject from 2014, Van Maren quotes from Bury the Chains by Adam Hochschild which talks about the decision of the Society for Effecting the Abolition of the Slave Trade to abolish the trade rather than go for emancipation. Van Maren's excerpt stops a few sentences short of the crucial line: "Abolishing the trade, Clarkson was certain, was 'laying the axe at the very root' of Caribbean slavery itself."

questions.question: What distinction does Silberman draw between the British abolitionists' approach and modern pro-life incrementalism?
questions.answer: He argues British abolitionists rejected dehumanizing increments — they would never have accepted a bill protecting only people who were 25 percent white, then 50 percent — whereas pro-life incrementalism does the equivalent by pegging protection to gestational age (24-week, 20-week, 15-week, heartbeat).
questions.quote: Had someone suggested protecting those who were 25 percent white, then 50 percent, then 75 percent, then abolition, it would have been roundly rejected by both American and British abolitionists. This is the sort of incrementalism pro-lifers practice with 24-week bans, 20-week bans, 15-week bans, heartbeat bills, etc.

questions.question: Why does Silberman defend the abolitionist coinage 'overnightism'?
questions.answer: He concedes abolitionists made the word, but says they did so to label a straw man invented by incrementalists — the caricature that immediatists believe abortion can be ended overnight by 'snapping fingers' — so that the difference between immediatism and that caricature could be clearly named.
questions.quote: Overnightism is a word we made up, but it is a concept pro-life incrementalists made up. They straw man immediatism as being an ideology where you can simply "snap your fingers" or "wave a magic wand" and abortion is gone overnight without any hard work or faithfulness.

questions.question: What parallel does Silberman draw between Henry Dundas and modern pro-life leaders?
questions.answer: He cites Dundas's 1792 speech professing agreement with Wilberforce's end goal while opposing immediate abolition on grounds of prudence and practicability, and argues this is the identical posture taken today by Van Maren, Klusendorf, Abby Johnson, and Right to Life.
questions.quote: Wait a second. Dundas agreed with Wilberforce's end goal but opposed immediate abolition because he disagreed with the abolitionists as to what was the most effective and practicable strategy? Where have we heard that exact argument before? Ah, right. Van Maren. And Klusendorf. And Abby. And Right to Life. The Pro-Life Movement clearly – clearly! – is Dolben and Dundas, not Wilberforce and Clarkson.

questions.question: How does Silberman use Van Maren's own podcast statement about the Dolben Act against him?
questions.answer: He notes that Van Maren explicitly called the Dolben Act 'the brainchild' of pro-life clinic-regulation strategy, then shows from Wilberforce's own writing that Wilberforce condemned the Dolben Act as ultimately beneficial to slaveholders — meaning Van Maren has, by his own analogy, identified pro-life strategy with what Wilberforce opposed.
questions.quote: Van Maren is on record calling the Dolben Act "the brainchild" of the pro-life political strategy, and Wilberforce is on record opposing the Dolben Act.

questions.question: How does Silberman respond to Van Maren's 'And then what?' challenge about federal pushback against state abolition bills?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing an abolition bill
questions.answer: He says abolitionists fully expect pushback — likely economic sanctions under a Democratic administration, conceivably even federal force — but argues the question presupposes cowardice abolitionists do not have, and insists they will not retreat regardless of cost.
questions.quote: BABIES ARE BEING MURDERED. You can put a gun to ours heads and we won't budge from demanding abortion's total and immediate abolition.

questions.question: What statistic does Silberman use to argue that pro-life messaging has produced a watered-down movement?
questions.answer: He cites Gallup showing 46 percent self-identify as pro-life while Pew shows only 12 percent want abortion illegal in all cases, meaning only roughly one-in-four pro-lifers actually wants total abolition — a result he attributes to bills like pain-capable acts that teach pain-capability rather than humanity as the criterion for protection.
questions.quote: While it is true that pro-life vs. pro-choice polls remain roughly even, with 46 percent of the country identifying as pro-life, only 12 percent support abortion being illegal in all cases. That means that only one-in-four pro-lifers desires to see abortion completely abolished. This is the fruit of pro-life compromise.

questions.question: What evidence does Silberman give that the Abolitionist Movement was not in decline during the years Van Maren claims Russell Hunter needed to 'resurrect' his influence?
questions.answer: He points to the first abolition bill in 2016, abolition bills in nine states over the next four years, the 2018 Dan Fisher gubernatorial campaign, the 2019 Oklahoma Southern Baptist and Free Will Baptist resolutions, and a 2020 capitol rally of nearly 3,000 people.
questions.quote: Russell joined the Dan Fisher for Governor campaign in 2018 which put the Abolitionist Movement on the map politically in Oklahoma. Free the States was founded at the conclusion of the Fisher campaign. 2019 is when the Oklahoma Southern Baptists and Free Will Baptists passed resolutions demanding abortion's immediate abolition without any compromise. 2020 saw the biggest abolitionist rally ever with almost 3,000 people at the Oklahoma State Capitol.

questions.question: Why does Silberman call Van Maren's framing of bloodguiltiness as 'oddly catholic' theologically problematic?
questions.answer: He argues that treating repentance — including national or corporate repentance for systemic sin — as Roman Catholic penance betrays an antinomian instinct, citing Luther's first thesis that the entire life of believers is meant to be one of repentance.
questions.quote: That Van Maren thinks Christians and nations repenting of sin is a Roman Catholic doctrine makes me worried about his understanding of sin and repentance. The very first of Martin Luther's 95 theses was, "Our Lord and Master Jesus Christ. willed the entire life of believers to be one of repentance."

questions.question: How does Silberman rebut Van Maren's claim that the Pro-Life Movement is the first human rights movement to lack press support?
questions.answer: He counters that the slavery abolitionists had only their own publications — The Liberator, The North Star, The Genius of Universal Emancipation — while the modern Pro-Life Movement is regularly published in conservative outlets and even mainstream papers like the New York Times, USA Today, and Bloomberg. He quotes Garrison's 1858 'Depravity of the American Press' to show abolitionists faced near-total press hostility.
questions.quote: The Pro-Life Movement has the support of every conservative outlet, and pro-lifers are regularly published in mainstream leftist publications such as the New York Times, USA Today, Bloomberg, and others. Of the fact that the vast majority of the mainstream press is pro-child sacrifice, there can be no reasonable doubt. But pro-lifers still enjoy far, far greater media support than what the abolitionists of slavery enjoyed.

questions.question: What kind of abolition bill does Silberman offer as one Van Maren should be willing to support?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing an abolition bill
questions.answer: He points to Idaho H0056, an abolition bill that does not formally repeal pro-life regulations but supersedes them by directing the attorney general to enforce the new chapter, neutralizing the standard pro-life objection that an abolition bill would wipe out existing restrictions.
questions.quote: ID H0056 is one such example. It repeals only one section of the Idaho homicide statutes which protects legal abortion. It does not officially repeal pro-life regulations. It simply supersedes them while leaving them on the books by establishing that "Notwithstanding any other provision of law, the attorney general shall monitor this state's enforcement of this chapter in relation to abortion."

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue that pain-capable bans actively miseducate the public about abortion?
questions.answer: He says pain-capable bans frame the conversation around the horror of aborting pain-capable children rather than the humanity of all preborn children, teaching people that pain-capability is the basis for legal protection — and offers an anecdote of a woman entering an abortion clinic saying her baby would not feel pain.
questions.quote: Pain-capable bans do not create a conversation about the horror of abortion, they create a conversation about the horror of aborting pain-capable children. These laws establish pain-capability as the basis for protection under the law. Beyond being fundamentally unjust, this educates the public that pain-capability is what makes humans worthy of protection. This is what led a young woman to walk into the Norman child sacrifice center in 2016 saying, "It's okay. My baby won't even feel any pain."

questions.question: Why does Silberman say abolitionists discuss criminal liability for women at all, given Van Maren's 'camping out' accusation?
questions.answer: He says abolitionists only address it because pro-lifers make automatic immunity one of their primary objections, and lists four reasons such immunity is wrong: it denies equal protection, allows self-induced abortion, removes deterrence, and undercuts the anti-abortion argument both morally and legally.
questions.quote:
We wouldn't dwell on the topic a single minute if it weren't for pro-lifers making this one of their biggest objections to abolition bills. So, often, we must explain why giving women automatic immunity in the murder of their children:

1. Is fundamentally unjust by denying equal protection to the preborn child,
2. allows self-induced abortion,
3. provides no deterrent against abortion, and
4. undercuts the anti-abortion argument morally and legally.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer the coercion objection to fully criminalizing abortion?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing an abolition bill
questions.answer: He notes that abolition bills already incorporate the longstanding legal principle that those acting under involuntary subjection are not capable of committing crimes, citing the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act's restatement of existing law.
questions.quote: The Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, for instance, restates existing law which establishes that "Persons who committed the act, or make the omission charged, while under involuntary subjection to the power of superiors" are not capable of committing crimes.

questions.question: How does Silberman correct Van Maren's use of Blackstone's Ratio?
questions.answer: He argues Blackstone's Ratio is properly a counsel to jurors and judges to require certainty before convicting — quoting John Adams on the catastrophe of condemning the innocent — and is not an argument against passing legislation, since by that logic no laws could exist.
questions.quote: In its correct context, Blackstone's Ratio is an argument for cautiousness on the part of jurors and judges. They must not convict a man of a crime unless they are absolutely certain of his guilt because the consequences of a wrongful conviction are enormous... But Blackstone's Ratio cannot be employed against legislation, and certainly not against legislation which makes all humans equal under law. If it can, then all laws must be done away with because they make possible the prosecution and conviction of the innocent.

questions.question: How does Silberman answer the claim that no single law can address the hundred different reasons women have abortions?
questions.answer: He argues a prospective murder charge would deter the vast majority of those women — and would also deter the upstream immorality that leads to abortion by removing it as a safety net — and frames this as the Romans 13 obligation of government to be a terror to evildoers.
questions.quote: A prospective murder charge would have disincentivized every single one of those women from murdering their sons and daughters. A handful likely would have murdered their child anyway, but the vast majority wouldn't. Even better, many men and women wouldn't be so irresponsible and immoral if they didn't have abortion as a safety net. That murder charge doesn't only disincentivize abortion, it would disincentivize the immorality that leads to abortion.

questions.question: How does Silberman address the claim that varying degrees of moral culpability among post-abortive women preclude criminalization?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing an abolition bill
questions.answer: He affirms the premise but says varying culpability is exactly what prosecutors, jurors, and judges exist to weigh case-by-case. The legislature's role is the equal treatment of all human beings under law, which only an abolition bill accomplishes.
questions.quote: But varying degrees of culpability do not mean we refrain from criminalizing murder, theft, assault, fraud, etc. It means that prosecutors and jurors must treat every instance individually, taking into account all context relevant to the crime and making their prosecutorial and sentencing decisions accordingly. Prosecutors, jurors, and judges sort out the details of each case. The role of the legislator is to treat all human beings equally under the law.

questions.question: Where does Silberman locate the actual origin of abolitionism, and what does he credit Russell Hunter and the Abolitionist Movement with specifically?
questions.answer: He says abolitionism originates in Scripture — Genesis through Revelation — and was practically applied by the slavery abolitionists. Russell and the post-2010 movement did not invent the doctrine but were the first to comprehensively apply it to abortion, with Senator Joseph Silk filing the first true abolition bill in 2016.
questions.quote: Is that to say abolitionism originated with Russell? Of course not. It originates in Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Proverbs, Isaiah, Amos, Ezekiel, Matthew, Romans, Acts, Ephesians, Revelation, and many other books of scripture. These principles were then put into practice by the abolitionists of slavery. But they were not comprehensively applied to the abortion battle until Russell and the abolitionists came on the scene in 2010.

related.title: Jonathan Van Maren and Josh Brahm. Yikes.
related.reason: The original Silberman article that this piece is the follow-up to; Van Maren's partial response triggered this rebuttal.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Lays out the systematic abolitionist case against incrementalism that this article applies to Van Maren's specific defenses.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Another Free the States rebuttal of a prominent incrementalist's misuse of Wilberforce/Garrison history, parallel in method to this piece.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the critique of pro-life submission to Roe that Silberman invokes when calling submission to Roe v. Wade sin.

related.title: Immediatism
related.reason: Defines the immediatist position Silberman defends against Van Maren's 'overnightism' straw man.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Explains the criminalization and equal-protection framework underlying Silberman's responses to the coercion, Blackstone's Ratio, and 'punishing women' objections.
